skill.name: email-sequence
skill.description: When the user wants to create or optimize an email sequence, drip campaign, automated email flow, or lifecycle email program. Also use when the user mentions "email sequence," "drip campaigns," or automated email marketing.
skill.summary: Create effective email sequences that nurture relationships, drive action, and convert leads into customers.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static security findings are false positives. The detected 'external_commands' are markdown code block fences, not shell execution. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'reconnaissance' patterns are triggered by innocent keywords in marketing content. This is a legitimate email marketing skill with no malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Email sequences often fail due to poor strategy and generic content. This skill provides expert guidance on building drip campaigns that nurture relationships and drive conversions.
actual_capabilities: Design complete email sequences from trigger to conversion
Create targeted drip campaigns for different audience segments
Write compelling subject lines and email copy that drives action
Set optimal timing and delays between emails in a sequence
Build re-engagement campaigns to win back inactive subscribers
Define clear metrics and success criteria for email sequences
limitations: Does not send emails - only provides strategy and copy
Cannot integrate with specific email platforms without user input
Does not have access to real subscriber data or analytics
Cannot test or optimize sequences in real-time

use_cases.title: Marketing Team Lead
use_cases.description: Build automated email nurture sequences to guide leads through the sales funnel without manual outreach.

use_cases.title: Small Business Owner
use_cases.description: Create welcome series and post-purchase sequences to engage customers and increase repeat sales.

use_cases.title: Content Creator
use_cases.description: Design educational email courses that deliver value and build audience trust over time.
use_cases.target_user: Bloggers and creators building email lists

prompt_templates.title: Basic Sequence Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers to our [industry] newsletter. The goal is to build trust and introduce our main offer.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with a simple email sequence

prompt_templates.title: Segmented Campaign
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a lead nurture sequence for users who signed up for our free [tool/resource] but have not converted. Create 3 different versions based on user intent: [intent A], [intent B], and [intent C].
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating targeted content for different audience segments

prompt_templates.title: Re-engagement Series
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a 3-email re-engagement sequence for subscribers who have been inactive for 60+ days. Include a special incentive in the final email.

prompt_templates.title: Full Campaign Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comprehensive email sequence strategy for our [product/service]. Include: sequence type recommendation, optimal length and timing, subject line strategy, segmentation approach, and key metrics to track. Our target audience is [description] and primary goal is [goal].
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a complete email marketing strategy

output_examples.input: Create a welcome sequence for a SaaS product
output: Email 1: Welcome & Immediate Value (Day 0) - Subject: Welcome to [Product]! Here's your quick-start guide
Email 2: Feature Deep Dive (Day 2) - Subject: The feature most users miss
Email 3: Social Proof (Day 4) - Subject: How [Customer Type] achieved [Result]
Email 4: Case Study (Day 7) - Subject: The exact strategy that got [Client] a 40% lift
Email 5: Soft Ask (Day 10) - Subject: Quick question about your goals
Email 1: Reminder (1 hour) - Subject: You left something behind...
Email 2: Objection Handling (24 hours) - Subject: Questions? Let us help.
Email 3: Incentive (48 hours) - Subject: Last chance: 10% off

best_practices: Start with a clear trigger and defined entry/exit conditions for each sequence
Lead with value - give before asking for any commitment or purchase
Test one variable at a time: subject lines, send times, or copy elements
anti_patterns: Sending too many emails too quickly - respect subscriber inbox patience
Making every email a sales pitch - balance value with promotional content
Using generic, templated copy that does not reflect your brand voice

faq.question: How many emails should be in a sequence?
faq.answer: Sequence length depends on your goal and sales cycle. Welcome sequences work well with 3-7 emails. Lead nurture sequences typically need 5-10 emails spread over weeks. Post-purchase sequences can be 3-5 emails.

faq.question: What is the best timing between emails?
faq.answer: For welcome sequences, send the first email immediately and follow up 1-2 days apart. Early sequence emails should arrive 1-3 days apart. Long-term nurture can be weekly or bi-weekly. Always consider your audience preferences.

faq.question: How do I measure email sequence success?
faq.answer: Track open rates (target 20-30%), click-through rates (target 2-5%), conversion rates, unsubscribe rates, and revenue attributed to the sequence. Set up A/B tests for subject lines and content.

faq.question: Should I segment my email sequences?
faq.answer: Yes, segmentation significantly improves performance. Segment by source (what triggered entry), behavior (engagement level), demographics, or purchase history. Even simple segments like 'opened last email' vs 'did not open' improve results.

faq.question: How do I write effective subject lines?
faq.answer: Keep subject lines under 50 characters. Be specific and benefit-driven. Use questions to create curiosity or direct statements for clarity. Test emoji sparingly. Avoid all-caps and excessive punctuation.

faq.question: When should I remove subscribers from a sequence?
faq.answer: Remove subscribers who consistently do not engage (no opens after 5-7 emails), who explicitly unsubscribe, or who meet exit conditions like making a purchase. Quality of list matters more than quantity.
